summary: Gruber reacts to the release of Netscape 7.0 with dismissal and sadness. He questions why anyone would use Netscape 7 over Mozilla 1.1, since Netscape removed Mozilla's most popular feature — pop-up ad blocking — and replaced it with ads for AOL services. He sees this as both strategically foolish and a melancholy decline from Netscape's glory days, when new versions genuinely advanced the web.
key_points: Netscape 7.0 is based on Mozilla but strips out the most popular feature: pop-up ad blocking
Netscape not only removed ad-blocking but added its own ads for AOL services into the browser UI
There is no rational reason to choose Netscape 7 over Mozilla 1.1
The decline of Netscape from web pioneer to ad-laden AOL product is both absurd and tragic
money_quotes: Not only did they remove the ad-blocking — a feature that is popular because people are annoyed by stupid ads — but they junked up the browser UI by adding a bunch of their own ads for crappy AOL services.
This isn't just preposterously dumb — it's sad.
Especially so if you remember when Netscape was cool, back when a major new version of Netscape meant that the entire web instantly became a better place.
key_insight: Netscape's transformation from web pioneer to an ad-stuffed shell of Mozilla perfectly illustrates how corporate ownership can strip everything good from a product and replace it with everything users hate.
